frozen_components: Pretrained SAE encoder parameters (frozen during reward modeling)
LLM backbone layers up to the SAE insertion point
architecture.summary: SARM integrates a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) into a reward model to enable interpretability. The system operates in two stages: (1) a pretrained SAE on sequence-level hidden activations from an intermediate layer of an LLM extracts sparse, monosemantic features; (2) this SAE encoder is frozen and attached to the corresponding layer of a reward model, projecting hidden states into a sparse feature space from which a learnable linear value head aggregates feature activations into a scalar reward score. Information flows from LLM hidden states → SAE encoder (frozen) → sparse latent features → weighted linear aggregation → scalar reward.
trained_components: Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) encoder on sequence-level activations
Linear value head for aggregating SAE feature activations

biblio.title: Interpretable Reward Model via Sparse Autoencoder

claims.statement: SARM-4B achieves the best overall score across all models on RewardBench 2, outperforming both open- and closed-source baselines, including models with significantly larger parameter counts
Replacing the pretrained SAE encoder with a randomly initialized linear layer results in a notable drop in overall performance
Token-level SAE pretraining achieves an overall score of 71.5, improving upon the random-init baseline but falling short of full sequence-level SARM
SARM facilitates direct feature-level attribution of reward assignments, enabling transparent and conceptually meaningful reward scores traced back to sparse, interpretable monosemantic features

Manipulating safety-related features through value head weight adjustment causes a rightward shift in reward distribution on safety-focused data while leaving unrelated data largely unchanged, demonstrating causally controllable and interpretable preference manipulation

SARM extracts human-interpretable features including Positive Features (e.g., ethical reasoning, factual accuracy, technical competence) and Negative Features (e.g., harmful instructions, unethical advice, offensive language) that align with value head weights
SARM enables dynamic adjustment to preference shifts through direct modification of value head weights without requiring costly multidimensional annotations

Incorporating SAE pretraining introduces additional computational overhead during training compared to standard scalar reward models, and the unsupervised nature of SAE training offers no guarantees that extracted features will align with human-desired concepts
